title: They should teach patriotism in schools
gist: Though titled around teaching patriotism in schools, the visible text (cut short by the paywall) instead argues that elite pro-patriotism commentary from writers like Noah Smith and Jerusalem Demsas is too abstract, holding up Iowa gubernatorial candidate Rob Sand's habit of opening town halls by singing 'America the Beautiful' as a model for what concrete patriotic practice could look like.

themes.theme: Patriotism needs practice, not just takes

insights.claim: Yglesias argues that liberal commentary calling for a revival of American patriotism — citing Noah Smith's essay on liberal nationalism and Jerusalem Demsas's argument that you have to love America to save it — remains too abstract, lacking concrete practices for how patriotism should actually be embraced.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Identifies a gap between elite pro-patriotism discourse and actionable political practice — relevant to how Democratic candidates might differentiate themselves culturally.
insights.quote: how you have to love America in order to save it
insights.locator: I've also been in a bunch of conversations

insights.claim: Iowa gubernatorial candidate Rob Sand opens his town halls by having the room sing 'America the Beautiful,' a concrete patriotic ritual Yglesias cites as a model for operationalizing pro-America messaging.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A specific, replicable campaign tactic for Democratic candidates seeking to reclaim patriotic branding — worth watching whether other candidates adopt it.
insights.quote: open town halls by having everyone sing “America the Beautiful.”

insights.claim: A friend describes Rob Sand as 'the Daniel Day-Lewis of politics' for recognizing early that national Democratic Party trends and Iowa's political trends were diverging, and fully committing to a distinctive, unbroken 'post-partisan Iowa' persona that performs patriotism as natural rather than performative.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Offers a reusable archetype — full-immersion local persona-building — for evaluating which Democratic candidates can credibly compete in red or purple states.

insights.claim: Yglesias contends that if liberals genuinely want to embrace American patriotism, it must go beyond clicking 'like' and 'share' on columns arguing patriotism is desirable, implying performative online endorsement without real civic practice is insufficient.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A blunt critique useful for gauging whether pro-patriotism messaging from Democratic-aligned media is likely to translate into actual electoral behavior change.
insights.quote: that has to mean more than just clicking “like” and “share” on columns
insights.locator: But if liberals want to embrace
